Adding validation to lexicon has a couple of advantages:

- better usability, users know immediately if something is wrong
- better error messages

But there are also some disadvantages:

- our specified provider TTL range may be out-of-date with the actual API, will require a new version to be released. 
- having a lexicon specific TTL default breaks the [principle of least astonishment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment). Now instead of letting the API determine the default TTL if none is provided, 
lexicon will send its own default, which may not be expected. 
- if we decide to go down the route of client validation, we should also consider provider specific validation for record types, record content length, record character sets, and more.

If you don't wait on the future returned by `queue.put` before calling `fill` again, specifying a capacity when you construct the queue is useless. This won't slow down reading, it'll just keep queueing up futures in the IOLoop that will eventually `put` into the queue. You want to do something like this:

```python
self.queue.put(f.result()).add_done_callback(lambda f: io_loop.spawn_callback(self.fill))
# It's safe to use add_done_callback here because spawn_callback already delays the call
# until the next iteration of the IOLoop.
```

(Or also handle the exception for `queue.put`, although I'm not aware of any recoverable exceptions raised by `queue.put`.)

This test is still really long and complicated.  You want to have as little logic in a test case as possible.  For example, see some of these tests:
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4f8e04e6cdfbc8707707ac978a964777459c161e/tests/models/encoder_decoders/wikitables_semantic_parser_test.py#L316-L337
This test has about 20 lines, but a lot of those are comments, and there is no complex logic.  We're just instantiating some objects, calling a method, and asserting that the return value is what we expect.  The more logic you have in your test, the harder it is to understand, and the easier it is to not actually be testing what you think you're testing.

An example of what I meant by "construct a small world" is here:
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4f8e04e6cdfbc8707707ac978a964777459c161e/tests/models/encoder_decoders/wikitables_semantic_parser_test.py#L181-L224

Again this test looks long in number of lines, but there are no loops, and there's only one method that's called - the rest is just instantiating some objects that we use for the test, and those objects happen to be longish lists, so the test takes a bunch of lines.

A final example: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4f8e04e6cdfbc8707707ac978a964777459c161e/tests/nn/util_test.py#L71-L181

This test is checking a softmax function, which does something very similar to what you're doing.  The test just instantiates a tensor with made up numbers, calculates the expected values by hand, and assert that they end up correct.  No loops, no complex logic, just some simple examples that you can look at and know that they look right (there are also probably way more checks there than are necessary, but that's for historical reasons).

So, what I would recommend for this test is to construct a world with just a few entities, make up a `linking_scores` tensor with random numbers that are just given explicitly in a matrix (but _not_ with `torch.rand()`), and call `._get_linking_probabilities`, and have a check on the exact tensor that's returned.

Essentially the logic you are shooting for (in pseudocode) is:
```Python
# detect mismatch between example and supported options
for option in example_text:
  if option not in command.arguments.option_list:
    violations.append(option)
```

This will detect when an option or flag in the example text isn't actually supported by the command.  Then there's also this:

```Python
# detect when example is for wrong command
if not example_text.startswith(help_entry):
   violations.append(example_text)
```

An `exit` here is putting the responsibility in the wrong place. The plugin system should run each plugin-load in a `try`/`except` statement, catch any thrown exceptions and either exit (if the plugin is essential) or continue (if the plugin can just be disabled). It is not up to the plugin to decide that the whole of Ganga must shutdown because it failed to load.

To put it another way, there's a difference between library and application code. Library code (e.g. the LHCb plugin) should never call things like `exit` because you never know how the application (the Ganga runtime) is using it.

Until you do a transfer to the node, its state is considered 'unknown'. After that, a healthcheck() pings the node and updates the status.
We must either start healthcheck() by default for every neighbor, or not rely on the nodeaddresses_statuses at all. Otherwise it will be possible to have a scenario where the tx is (maliciously) routed through a more 'expensive' network (whatever 'expensive' means - be it length, total cost of tx, time...).

consider following topology

```
A +----> B ---+> C
  |           |
  +----> D ---+

```



A wants to do tx to C. Malicious actor B wants to increase his profit for relaying a transaction, so he will send a transfer to A (or to itself through A), thus making the AB channel monitored & in a 'reachable' state for node A. Honest actor D is also reachable, but at the moment he is not monitored and 'unknown' to A.

I wonder if we should be showing a hierarchical task tree view as much as possible, as like... a matter of principle almost. People coming to trio are used to unstructured global task lists in other systems (Unix processes, threads, asyncio, ...), and it's just not the right image to have in your head when you're trying to reason about a trio program, so reinforcing that seems like it might be important?

Also, as a usability thing, it'd probably be helpful to have a single command that dumps a combined task tree + stack traces of all tasks, for the use case where you're trying to do remote debugging via a confused user ("can you run the monitor, type <XX>, and paste the output you get into this issue?"). Sort of like how `thread apply all bt` is a magic incantation you ask people to type into gdb.

    1. Allow pre-1.1 implementations to simply infer the discriminator value for 1.0 files.
    2. Require 1.1 or later files to have the field written.  Absent (which defaults to 0 which is FLOAT) would always be treated as a FLOAT and any has_field heuristics would be illegal.

Given that, I'd suggest we simply bump the bugfix number in the version.
